---
title: "IC_R009: Bidirectional Commit-Issue Traceability Rule"
subtitle: "Every commit must reference an issue; every issue must have a resolving commit"
chapter: "CH06"
category: "rule"
number: "IC_R009"
date-created: "2026-03-07"
date-modified: "2026-03-07"
author: "Claude"
type: "rule"
law: "Integration & Collaboration Law Implementation Rules"
article-number: "Article 9"
implements:
  - "CH00.MP012: Change Tracking Principle"
related_to:
  - "IC_R008: GitHub Issue Scope Governance Rule"
  - "IC_P001: Universal Immediate Sync"
  - "SO_R029: CHANGELOG Organization Rule"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    number-sections: true
---

# Article Overview {#overview}

**Integration & Collaboration Law Implementation Rules Article 9 (Bidirectional Commit-Issue Traceability Rule)**

This article establishes a strict bidirectional link between Git commits and GitHub Issues. No commit may exist without a corresponding issue, and no issue may be closed without a corresponding commit.

:::{.callout-important}
## Core Rule
1. Every commit MUST reference at least one GitHub Issue number in its commit message.
2. Every GitHub Issue MUST have at least one commit that addresses it before being closed.
3. There are **no exceptions** — even single-line typo fixes require a corresponding issue.
:::

# Section 1: Commit → Issue Direction {#commit-to-issue}

## 1.1 Commit Message Format

Every commit message must include at least one issue reference:

```
[TYPE] Brief description (#NNN)

- Detail 1
- Detail 2

Closes #NNN
```

Or for commits that partially address an issue:

```
[TYPE] Brief description (refs #NNN)

- Detail 1
```

## 1.2 Acceptable Reference Formats

| Format | Meaning | When to Use |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `Closes #NNN` | Automatically closes issue on merge | Issue fully resolved by this commit |
| `Fixes #NNN` | Automatically closes issue on merge | Bug fix that resolves the issue |
| `refs #NNN` | References without closing | Partial work toward an issue |
| `(#NNN)` in title | Inline reference | Always include in title for quick scanning |

## 1.3 Multiple Issues

A single commit may reference multiple issues:

```
[FIX] Resolve translation and layout issues (#254, #256)

- Fix missing zh_TW keys for sidebar (#254)
- Correct CSS alignment in KPI cards (#256)

Closes #254, Closes #256
```

# Section 2: Issue → Commit Direction {#issue-to-commit}

## 2.1 Resolution Requirement

Before closing any GitHub Issue, at least one commit must exist that addresses it. This is verified by:

- The issue being auto-closed via `Closes #NNN` in a commit, OR
- A manual comment linking to the relevant commit SHA

## 2.2 No Close Without Code

Issues MUST NOT be closed with status "completed" unless there is a traceable commit. Acceptable alternatives:

| Scenario | Action |
|----------|--------|
| Issue resolved by code change | Close with commit reference |
| Issue is a duplicate | Close as "not planned" with link to original issue |
| Issue is no longer relevant | Close as "not planned" with explanation |
| Issue requires no code change (e.g., config-only in external system) | Document the action taken in a comment, then close |

# Section 3: No Exceptions Policy {#no-exceptions}

## 3.1 Why No Exceptions

Even small changes need an issue because:

1. **Auditability**: Without an issue, there is no record of *why* a change was made
2. **Reversibility**: If a "small fix" introduces a regression, the issue provides context for debugging
3. **Accountability**: Every change should be traceable to a decision or report
4. **Knowledge preservation**: "Why was this line changed?" is answered by the linked issue

## 3.2 Workflow for Small Fixes

For typo fixes, minor corrections, or small improvements:

1. Create a lightweight issue (title + one-line description is sufficient)
2. Make the fix
3. Commit with issue reference
4. Close the issue

Example:

```bash
# Step 1: Create issue
gh issue create \
  --repo kiki830621/ai_martech_global_scripts \
  --title "Fix typo in customerRetention KPI label" \
  --body "The label says 'Retenion' instead of 'Retention'." \
  --label "bug" \
  --label "priority:P3"

# Step 2: Fix and commit
git commit -m "[FIX] Correct typo in customerRetention label (#NNN)

Closes #NNN"
```

## 3.3 Batch Issues for Related Small Fixes

When multiple small fixes are discovered together, a single "housekeeping" issue covering all of them is acceptable:

```bash
gh issue create \
  --title "Housekeeping: fix typos and minor issues in VitalSigns module" \
  --body "- Fix label typo in customerRetention
- Correct CSS margin in revenuePulse
- Update outdated comment in customerAcquisition"
```

Then reference this single issue in one or more commits.

# Section 4: Enforcement {#enforcement}

## 4.1 Pre-Commit Verification

Before committing, verify:

- [ ] Is there a GitHub Issue for this change?
- [ ] Does the commit message include `#NNN`?
- [ ] If this is the final commit for an issue, does it include `Closes #NNN`?

## 4.2 Violation Handling

If a commit is discovered without an issue reference:

1. Create a retroactive issue describing what the commit did and why
2. Add a comment to the issue linking to the commit SHA
3. This is a process violation — take care to prevent recurrence

# Section 5: Rationale {#rationale}

This rule exists to:

- Establish complete bidirectional traceability between decisions (issues) and implementations (commits)
- Enable any team member to understand *why* any line of code was changed
- Support customer-facing reporting (e.g., `/suggestion-report`) that traces issues to resolutions
- Prevent orphan commits that cannot be explained months later
- Prevent orphan issues that appear unresolved despite having been fixed
